fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build that CI config generator tool we've been talking about. The idea is that each package in the repo just has a small config file describing what SDKs to target and what steps to run, and then this tool spits out the full Travis CI setup plus a shell runner script automatically. No more hand-editing that giant YAML every time someone adds a new package.

A few things I know are important: packages should be able to share work when they're doing the same thing (why run the same check twice?), and when packages disagree on step ordering we should catch that early and tell the developer clearly instead of silently generating something broken. There's also some logic around how different task names that end up doing the same underlying thing should be handled — kinda like the deduplication approach we used in that billing pipeline refactor.

The tool also needs to read an optional root-level config file for repo-wide settings, and it should complain loudly if someone references a step name that doesn't actually exist anywhere. Validation of the per-package files should happen before anything is written out.

One more thing — there's a flag for how dependencies get fetched in the generated script, default behavior vs the alternative mode. Make sure warnings about SDK version mismatches and unused SDK declarations still show up in the console output even when we do generate successfully.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in. For task naming, the important bit is that we only keep the bare task name when every package resolves that name to the exact same shell command. If the same task name like `dartfmt` points at different concrete commands in different packages, then each distinct command needs its own suffixed key like `dartfmt_0`, `dartfmt_1`, etc, and both the job script entries and the runner case branches should use those suffixed keys. That’s the same disambiguation behavior from Feature 2.2 disambiguate task keys logic, as implemented in the task-extraction/de-duplication engine module.

On stage ordering, we’re not picking an order package by package. The order comes from one global topological sort over the union of every package's ordered stage list. So if two packages declare the same pair in opposite order, that creates a cycle and we should stop immediately with a `user_exception` using exactly `message=Not all packages agree on \`stages\` ordering, found a cycle between the following stages: [<sorted stage names>]` and  with an empty `details` field. In that case nothing gets written out.

A couple more specifics on caches and job generation. In `.travis.yml`, cache directories always start with `$HOME/.pub-cache` as the first entry. After that, anything from package `cache.directories` that starts with `/` is treated as an absolute/repo-root path, included once across packages, deduplicated, and sorted. Relative paths get namespaced under the package directory, so `.dart_tool` in `pkg_a` becomes `pkg_a/.dart_tool`. Then the final list is sorted after the pub-cache entry, with absolute paths and namespaced relative paths interleaved in sort order. Also, jobs do not get auto-collapsed just because packages share the same stage, SDK, and task command. Each package still gets its own job entry unless `merge_stages` is configured in `mono_repo.yaml`, which is the only time we combine packages into one job with space-separated PKGS. Cache directories still aggregate the same way either way.

For `tool/travis.sh`, the shape is fixed: shebang + header comment, PKGS guard, argument count guard, EXIT_CODE=0, then a per-package loop with `pushd`, the dependency-resolution block, and then a per-task loop with a `case` statement. The case branches need to be in alphabetical order by task key, and the `*)` branch always goes last. One tiny UX thing the team asked about: in the console output, after we acknowledge the `.travis.yml` write and before we acknowledge the `travis.sh` write, always remind the developer to `chmod +x ./tool/travis.sh`.

On dependency fetching, the `useGet` boolean option default `false` is what controls the verb in the generated runner script, and it comes through as part of the JSON input to the execution adapter. When `false`, use `pub upgrade --no-precompile` and the failure text must be `pub upgrade failed`. When `true`, use `pub get --no-precompile` and the failure text must be `pub get failed`.

And on SDK validation, Feature 4 is specifically the `environment.sdk` check from each pubspec.yaml: parse the semver range there and compare pinned numeric SDK versions against it, then emit the per-package warnings when a version is incompatible. Those warnings should still show up the same way on successful generation too.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: generateConfig(Map<String, String> files, {bool useGet = false}) -> GeneratorResult

trigger_keywords: root config file
mono_repo.yaml keys
unknown top-level keys
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The root `mono_repo.yaml` file accepts only two top-level keys: `travis` (for custom CI passthrough settings) and `merge_stages` (list of stage names to merge across packages). Any other top-level key triggers a `config_parse` error with a YAML source excerpt pointing to the offending key. The `travis` block may contain any CI settings except reserved structural keys; if the `travis` block contains a `branches` key, the generator suppresses its default branch block. An empty or absent `mono_repo.yaml` produces default output.

trigger_keywords: stage reference validation
unknown stage in mono_repo
stage does not exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Every stage name referenced in `mono_repo.yaml` (whether under `travis.stages` or `merge_stages`) must exist in at least one package's `mono_pkg.yaml`. If a referenced stage name is not found in any package definition, the generator emits a `user_exception` error: `message=Error parsing mono_repo.yaml` and `details=Stage \`<name>\` was referenced in \`mono_repo.yaml\`, but it does not exist in any \`mono_pkg.yaml\` files.`

trigger_keywords: group tasks
per-group SDK override
unused top-level dart versions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A stage entry may use a `group` key to bundle multiple tasks into one job with its own `dart` SDK list, overriding the package-level SDK list for that group. Each group produces one job per SDK in the group's list, running all group tasks together. If the package declares a top-level `dart` list but every stage entry overrides it with a group-level `dart` list, the top-level values are unused; the generator emits a per-package advisory warning (indented two spaces under the package line): `  \`dart\` values (<unused values>) are not used and can be removed.` This warning appears in the console log but does not prevent output generation.

trigger_keywords: SDK version constraint check
pubspec sdk range
incompatible SDK warning
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a `pubspec.yaml` declares an `environment.sdk` version constraint, the generator checks every explicitly-pinned (non-symbolic) SDK version in the job list against it. SDK versions that fall outside the constraint still produce jobs, but a per-package advisory warning is emitted: `  There are jobs defined that are not compatible with the package SDK constraint (<constraint>): \`<version>\`.` Symbolic versions like `dev` and `stable` are never checked against the constraint.

trigger_keywords: no packages found
missing mono_pkg.yaml
package validation errors
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Package-level validation errors each produce specific messages: (1) No directory with a `mono_pkg.yaml` → `user_exception` with `message=No packages found.` and `details=Each target package directory must contain a \`mono_pkg.yaml\` file.` (2) File content is not a YAML map → `user_exception` with `message=The contents of \`<path>\` must be a Map.` and empty details. (3) Valid config but zero runnable tasks generated → `user_exception` with `message=No entries created. Check your nested \`mono_pkg.yaml\` files.` and empty details. All validation happens before any output files are written.

trigger_keywords: job sort order
job entry ordering
matrix job sequence
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Jobs in the `.travis.yml` matrix are sorted by: (1) stage order (global topological order), (2) SDK version string alphabetically/lexicographically within a stage, (3) environment string (PKGS value), (4) script string. The SDK version in the `dart:` field is quoted with double-quotes when it looks like a number (e.g. `dart: "2.1.1"`, `dart: "1.23.0"`) but not for symbolic names (e.g. `dart: dev`, `dart: stable`).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

  if [[ ${PUB_EXIT_CODE} -ne 0 ]]; then
    EXIT_CODE=1
    echo -e '\033[31mpub upgrade failed\033[0m'
    popd
    continue
  fi

  for TASK in "$@"; do
    echo
    echo -e "\033[1mPKG: ${PKG}; TASK: ${TASK}\033[22m"
    case ${TASK} in
    dartanalyzer)
      echo 'dartanalyzer .'
      dartanalyzer . || EXIT_CODE=$?
      ;;
    *)
      echo -e "\033[31mNot expecting TASK '${TASK}'. Error!\033[0m"
      EXIT_CODE=1
      ;;
    esac
  done

  popd
done

exit ${EXIT_CODE}

Every referenced stage must exist in at least one package's definition; referencing a stage that no package declares is reported as a configuration error naming the offending stage.
